content:
# 1 Peter Chapter 3 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 267 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1PE_003_page_001.jpg

1. Likewise, the wives, being subject to the own husbands, so that even if some are disobedient to the word, through the conduct of the wives they will be gained without a word,

2. having observed the pure conduct of you in fear.

3. Of whom let it be not the outward braiding of hair and putting on of gold [ornaments] or wearing of garments [as] adornment,

4. but the hidden person of the heart, in the incorruptible [beauty] of the gentle and quiet spirit, which is before the God of great value.

5. For thus also formerly the holy women, the [ones] hoping in God, adorned themselves, being subject to the own husbands,

6. as Sarah obeyed the Abraham, calling him lord, of whom you became children, doing good and not fearing any terror.

7. The husbands likewise, dwelling together according to knowledge, as with a weaker vessel — the feminine [one] — assigning honor, as also co-heirs of [the] grace of life, so that the prayers of you are not hindered.

8. And the end [of it all]: [be] all like-minded, sympathetic, loving [as] brothers, compassionate, humble-minded,

9. not returning evil for evil, or reviling for reviling, but on the contrary blessing, because for this you were called, that you might inherit a blessing.

10. For "the [one] wanting to love life and to see good days, let him cease the tongue of him from evil, and [the] lips of him not to speak deceit;

11. and let him turn away from evil and let him do good; let him seek peace and let him pursue it.

12. Because [the] eyes of [the] Lord [are] upon [the] righteous, and [the] ears of him [are] toward [the] prayer of them; but [the] face of [the] Lord [is] against [the ones] doing evil."

13. And who [is] the [one] who will harm you if you become zealots of the good?

14. But even if you should suffer on account of righteousness, blessed [are you]. And the fear of them do not fear, nor be troubled,

15. but sanctify the Lord the Christos in the hearts of you, always ready toward a defense to everyone asking you a word concerning the hope in you, but with gentleness and fear,

16. having a good conscience, so that in what you are spoken against, the [ones] reviling the good conduct of you in Christos may be put to shame.

17. For [it is] better to suffer doing good, if the will of the God should will [it], than doing evil.

18. Because also Christos once concerning sins suffered, [the] righteous [one] on behalf of [the] unrighteous, so that he might bring you to the God, having been put to death indeed in [the] flesh, but having been made alive in [the] Spirit,

19. in which also to the spirits in prison having gone, he proclaimed,

20. [to the ones] having been disobedient formerly, when the patience of the God waited in [the] days of Noah, [while the] ark was being prepared, into which few — that is, eight souls — were saved through water.

21. Which [as] antitype now also saves you — baptism — not [the] putting away of [the] filth of [the] flesh, but [the] appeal of a good conscience toward God, through [the] resurrection of Yeshua Christos,

22. who is at [the] right [hand] of the God, having gone into heaven, angels and authorities and powers having been subjected to him.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — "the wives" (αἱ γυναῖκες):** Sinaiticus reads ὁμοίως αἱ γυναῖκες ("likewise the wives"), continuing the household code from 2:18. The ὁμοίως links the instruction to the broader pattern of submission under authority established in 2:13.

2. **v.6 — "the Abraham" (τῷ Ἀβραάμ):** Sinaiticus includes the definite article with Abraham, a standard Greek construction with well-known proper names. Sarah's obedience to Abraham (Gen 18:12) is cited as the model.

3. **v.7 — "as with a weaker vessel — the feminine" (ὡς ἀσθενεστέρῳ σκεύει τῷ γυναικείῳ):** The dative τῷ γυναικείῳ stands in apposition to σκεύει. "Vessel" (σκεῦος) here denotes the physical body, not a derogatory term; the comparative "weaker" is physical, not moral or intellectual.

4. **v.13 — "zealots of the good" (τοῦ ἀγαθοῦ ζηλωταί):** Sinaiticus reads ζηλωταὶ τοῦ ἀγαθοῦ. Some manuscripts read μιμηταί ("imitators") instead. The Sinaiticus reading "zealots" is the harder and likely original reading.

5. **v.15 — "the Lord the Christos" (τὸν κύριον τὸν Χριστόν):** Sinaiticus reads κύριον τὸν Χριστόν ("the Lord, the Christ"), where other manuscripts read κύριον τὸν θεόν ("the Lord God"), echoing Isaiah 8:13. The Sinaiticus reading applies the Isaianic Yahweh text directly to Christ.

6. **v.18 — "suffered" (ἔπαθεν):** Sinaiticus reads ἔπαθεν ("suffered") rather than ἀπέθανεν ("died") found in many later manuscripts. The reading "suffered" is strongly supported by the earliest witnesses and fits Peter's sustained theme of suffering throughout the epistle.

7. **v.18 — "you" (ὑμᾶς):** Sinaiticus reads ὑμᾶς ("you"), whereas some manuscripts read ἡμᾶς ("us"). The second-person pronoun directly addresses the readers and is the preferred Sinaiticus reading.

8. **v.19 — "in which also... he proclaimed" (ἐν ᾧ καὶ... ἐκήρυξεν):** One of the most debated passages in the New Testament. The "spirits in prison" (v.19-20) are most naturally read as the disobedient generation of Noah's time. Whether Christ preached in his pre-existent state through Noah, or descended after death, has been debated since the patristic era. The literal Sinaiticus text does not resolve this ambiguity.

9. **v.21 — "antitype" (ἀντίτυπον):** The flood-water is the type; baptism is the antitype (ἀντίτυπον). Peter immediately clarifies that baptism saves not by physical washing but by the appeal (ἐπερώτημα) of a good conscience — the inward reality the water symbolizes.

10. **v.22 — "having gone into heaven":** The verse concludes with Christ's exaltation at God's right hand with all cosmic powers subjected. This forms an arc with v.18: suffering, death, resurrection, proclamation, ascension, and enthronement.

— EMET, AI Translator
